Black strikes back at White's center, opening up the light-squared bishop. This is the main branching point of the mainline Meran, since White has to choose between one of two pawn breaks: e5 and d5.
White strikes in the center, opening up the dark-squared bishop in the process. Black's main move is /9.
Black protects the b-pawn so they can play 9. c5 without blundering a pawn.
White returns the bishop to its original position, from where it supports the e4 pawn push. This is considered to be the main line.

Black expands on the queenside and makes the b7-square available for their bishop while gaining a tempo on White's bishop. White should retreat the bishop, there are three viable options:

/8.
White recaptures the pawn. b5/ is the most proper response, justifying the loss of control over the center by making the b7-square available for the light-squared bishop while gaining a tempo.
Black captures the c4 pawn with a tempo on White's bishop, forcing White to move it again. Bxc4/ is the only sensible move.
White intends to enter the Meran variation. However, Black gets the final say in this matter.

This is a waiting move: Black is waiting for White to develop the light-squared bishop with /6. Black does so in order to win some important tempi on White's bishop to make space for Black's light-squared bishop on b7.
This move protects the c4-pawn with the light-squared bishop, but keeps the dark-squared bishop behind the pawn-chain. Play is usually very positional compared to the 5.
A highly theoretical opening. Black willingly keeps the light-squared bishop behind the pawn-chain, intending to free it later with either the e5 or c5 pawn breaks.
#REDIRECT Chess Opening Theory/1.
Black develops a knight to f6, and puts pressure on the e4-square. From here White has multiple good responses, although 4.

White uses the fact that the capture has left the e4-square undefended and expends their centre, revealing an attack from the light-squared bishop on the c4 pawn. Black generally holds onto their pawn with /4.

This move captures the gambit pawn. Since White's knight on c3 is exposed to attacks by Black's b-pawn, Black can often safely over-extend with moves like a6 and b5 to keep a hold of the c-pawn in this line.
e4 impacts the center, shaping the pawn structure in the Queen's Gambit.
Black offers a gambit pawn in order to develop more quickly, as well as displacing White's knight. If the gambit is accepted, Black usually regains the pawn but more than returns the time: after 4.

#Redirect Chess Opening Theory/1.
e3 reinforces the center solidly, maintaining flexibility in the Queen's Gambit.
e5 advances a pawn, influencing the structure in the Queen's Gambit.
b5 advances a pawn, influencing the structure in the Queen's Gambit.
White occupies the center with his pawns. In doing so, Black gets the chance to hold onto the c4-pawn with the move b5, expanding on the queenside.
Black develops the knight to a6 in order to later transfer it to b4, which was weakened by White's move 5. This does however give White time to occupy the center with 6.
e6 advances a pawn, influencing the structure in the Queen's Gambit.
Instead of the usual 5. Bf5, Black opts to put the bishop on g4, harassing White's knight.
White immediately goes after Black's light-squared bishop. The usual response is 6.
g5 is the most popular line nowadays and is considered the best move for Black. Because the kingside pawn structure is significantly weakened, Black will usually resort to castling queenside.
White develops the light-squared bishop and prepares to castle short. From this position, two moves have been played historically: f6 and g5.
This move defends the pinned knight. White no longer has any moves to increase the pressure on the e5 knight, so play usually continues by developing the light-squared bishop with 11.
This move pins the knight on e5 to the black queen, which is its only defender at the moment. Moving the knight isn't an option, so the only move to add another defender is 10.
The central pawns have been exchanged. White can, and should, play 10.
White exchanges the pawns in the center. Black doesn't have many valid options other than 9.
Black finally pushes the e pawn forward, releasing the dark-squared bishop and fighting for the center. If White ignores the pawn and prepares to castle with 9.
This move prepares to develop both bishops; the dark-squared bishop to f4 and the light-squared bishop to g2. This move doesn't deter Black from playing /8.
Black develops the queen to c7 in order to prepare e5. This will likely lead to the Carlsbad variation.
White recaptures the c4 pawn. Black can now choose to either enter the Carlsbad variation with 7.
This is a simple developing move. White's only real follow-up is 7.

Black prepares to develop their dark-squared bishop to b4 where it stands uncontested thanks to White's move 5. If White tries to recapture the c4 pawn immediately with 7.
White places their knight into the center to recapture the pawn on c4 while leaving the diagonal for the dark-squared bishop open. There are two theoretical responses for Black.

White is threatening to play 10. e4 with a tempo on the bishop.
Black quickly castles their king to safety. This gives Black the option of playing 9.
White castles their king to safety to unpin the knight and to start preparing e4. Black can now choose to either play /8.
Black develops the bishop to its most natural square. White should simply castle kingside /8.
White reclaims their lost pawn. Black should follow up with /7.
Black prepares to develop the dark-squared bishop to the b4-square, which was weakened by White's move 5. White can now simply recapture the c-pawn with /7.
White opens up the light-squared bishop in order to recapture the pawn on c4. In doing so, the movement of the dark-squared bishop is temporarily restricted in the hopes to open it up later with e4.
Black develops their light-squared bishop. This is possible now because the threat of Qb3 is no longer present thanks to the pawn on c4.
The advance of the a-pawn prevents Black from playing 5. b5, which in turn leaves the c4 pawn available for capture.
Black finally takes the sacrificial pawn with dxc4. Despite being known as the "Pure Slav," this move has about half the popularity of 4.

The most common move for Black. White now has to reveal their opening plan.
White doesn't reveal their intentions yet and simply develops the knight to its most natural square, putting pressure on d5. Black's main response is /4.

This is Black's best option, accepting a symmetrical position. While normal development moves will suffice, White can try to make it harder for Black to get the light-squared bishop out by delaying the combination of Nf3 and e3, which would allow Black to pin the knight to the queen with Bg4.
After this move, the tension in the center is relieved often allowing Black equality due to the symmetrical position. Because of this, this variation often ends in a draw.

Originally considered a less-orthodox defence in the Queen's Gambit, this opening has stood as an entire opening system in its own right for decades. The idea behind this defence is straightforward: instead of locking in the light squared bishop on c8, why not support the center with the c-pawn instead.

Arising from the Austrian Defense, the Gusev Countergambit, named after the Soviet Master of Sports Nikolai Nikolaevich Gusev, is represented by the gambit of a pawn on c5 with 3. In distinction to traditional openings that prioritize immediate control, Black elects to relinquish central pawns for active piece play.
The a logical and principled way of dealing with this central tension, using a flank pawn to exchange a center pawn. Black usually replies with 3.

The Austrian chess players Hans Haberditz (c. 1901–57), Hans Müller (1896–1971), and GM Ernst Grünfeld studied this defense, which was initially introduced by Italian chess player Alessandro Salvio in 1604.

Black strikes back at the center, potentially leaving White with an isolated queen's pawn after. White's main option is /6.
White recaptures the pawn while developing the light-squared bishop. This also allows White to quickly castle kingside.
This move prepares to release the f8-bishop into action. The light-squared bishop is supposed to go to b7, but sometimes has to settle for the passive d7-square.
This move prepares to develop the kingside and win c4.

Black develops and prevents White from establishing a pawn center with e4. |name2=Showalter variation
|line2=4.
This is the classical continuation. White pursues its development and increases control over the e5 square.

The Queen's Gambit Accepted has a rich heritage in chess, both sides played by many of the world champions through the years. It is not really much of a gambit since White can recover the pawn immediately with 3.
The Queen's gambit is one of the oldest openings in chess. White offers a pawn in order to gain control of the centre.
d5 is the classical approach to fighting for the centre: occupying it with pawns. d4 takes some space for Black and opens a line of development for Black's queenside bishop.
d4 is the Queen's pawn opening. d4 White looks to take over control of the centre.
